summary: The notebook demonstrates how to fine-tune the BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5 embedding model for a mathematics misconception mining task. It constructs training triplets by combining question metadata and answer text as anchors, correct misconceptions as positives, and top-25 retrieved misconceptions as negatives. The author uses cosine similarity with the base model to generate retrieval data, then fine-tunes the model using the sentence_transformers library and Triplet Loss. Readers should learn how to automate triplet generation for semantic retrieval fine-tuning and configure the SentenceTransformerTrainer for efficient training.
competition_flow: Loads raw CSVs, constructs anchor/positive/negative triplets via top-k cosine retrieval, and fine-tunes the BGE embedding model using Triplet Loss.
data_reading: Reads train.csv and misconception_mapping.csv using Polars.
data_processing: Unpivots wide answer and misconception columns to long format.
Concatenates metadata and answer text into anchor strings.
Computes top-25 cosine similarity retrievals using the base BGE model.
Filters identical positive/negative pairs and converts to HuggingFace Dataset format.
insights: Top-k cosine similarity retrieval with a base embedding model can effectively generate negative samples for triplet training.
Concatenating structured metadata and answer text creates a robust anchor for semantic matching.
The SentenceTransformerTrainer simplifies fine-tuning by handling batch samplers, gradient accumulation, and logging out of the box.
